# Old Theory to Modern Video Translation

## AIDA → First-frame system

```text
Attention → first frame
Interest → hook
Desire → proof/payoff
Action → save/send/comment/follow CTA
```

Tactic:

```text
Design the first frame before the full script.
```

## Information foraging → Information scent

Viewers estimate value versus effort.

Tactic:

```text
Make the topic, target viewer, and payoff obvious in the first 1–2 seconds.
```

Bad:

```text
“This one trick changes everything.”
```

Better:

```text
“Where to place captions so they do not cover the subject.”
```

## Processing fluency → Visual clarity

Easier-to-process content feels more credible and watchable.

Tactic:

```text
Use one dominant subject, readable text, clean negative space, and clear visual hierarchy.
```

## Dual coding → Verbal/visual alignment

Words and visuals should divide labour.

```text
visual = show the thing
text = name the thing
voice = explain the meaning
caption = preserve speech
annotation = direct attention
```

## Information-gap theory → Hook tension

Curiosity comes from a specific missing answer.

Tactic:

```text
Create a gap the video can close quickly.
```

Good:

```text
“This caption style looks clean, but watch where attention drops.”
```

Weak:

```text
“You won’t believe this.”
```

## Framing theory → Metric shaping

The same lesson can become a mistake, myth, checklist, teardown, experiment, warning, or transformation.

Metric tendencies:

```text
mistake frame → early retention
checklist frame → saves
teardown frame → follows
myth-bust frame → comments
experiment frame → completion
identity frame → sends
```

## Behaviour design → CTA design

Behaviour happens when motivation, ability, and prompt converge.

Tactic:

```text
The CTA must match the value created by the video.
```

Examples:

```text
Checklist → “Save this before your next edit.”
Identity joke → “Send this to your editor friend.”
Comparison → “A or B?”
Series → “Follow the lab for the next test.”
```

## Diffusion theory → Adoptability

A tactic spreads when it is easy to understand, compatible with existing workflows, observable, and easy to test.

Tactic:

```text
Turn abstract advice into a concrete next-edit action.
```

## Weak ties → Shareable recipient clarity

Content travels when it can be sent across clusters.

Tactic:

```text
Name the recipient: “Send this to someone who...”
```

## Social proof → Proof assets

People trust what they can see others respond to or what has visible evidence.

Proof assets:

```text
real comment
before/after
analytics screenshot
retention graph
screen recording
live edit
case study
```

## Peak-end rule → Ending design

The final beat heavily shapes remembered value.

Tactic:

```text
End with a rule, reveal, checklist, loop, or series promise.
```
